title: Art, Extractivism and the Ontological Shift: Toward a (Post)Extractivist Aesthetics
author: Serafini

opening_move.description: The paper opens by diagnosing a gap: while there is growing scholarship on extractivism as an economic and political phenomenon, the aesthetic and cultural dimensions of resistance to extractivism remain theoretically underdeveloped. Serafini argues that art practices in affected communities constitute a distinct form of political ontology that cannot be captured by existing frameworks.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A (post)extractivist aesthetics is not merely a cultural supplement to political resistance but constitutes an ontological shift — art enacts alternative worlds rather than representing them, and theorizing this is politically urgent.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper builds a framework of 'prefigurative ontological design' by synthesizing Escobar's designs for the pluriverse, Wynter's autopoiesis, Blaser's political ontology, and ethnographic data from Argentine art collectives. It engages critically with relational aesthetics and participatory art theory (Kester, Bishop) as insufficiently ontological, positioning itself as extending the decolonial political ontology tradition into aesthetics.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Multi-sited ethnographic fieldwork in Argentina (2017–2019); 60+ semi-structured interviews with artists, activists, and community members; participant observation; analysis of artworks, murals, and collective mapping practices.
data_sources: Fieldwork in Andalgalá (Catamarca), Buenos Aires, Córdoba
Murals of anti-mining resistance in Andalgalá
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Political ontology framework (Blaser, Escobar); decolonial aesthetics; prefigurative politics theory. The theoretical concept of 'prefigurative ontological design' is generated inductively from the fieldwork cases and then elaborated theoretically.
epistemic_mode.notes: The ethnographic evidence is constitutive: the key theoretical contribution — prefigurative ontological design — is derived from the empirical cases, not merely illustrated by them. The paper could not arrive at this concept through purely theoretical work; the concept is generated by the encounter between theory and fieldwork. This makes the empirical evidence constitutive rather than illustrative, despite the paper's strong normative orientation.

case_type.case_name: Argentine anti-extractivist art collectives (La Ala Plástica, Revolución CC / Casa 1234, Iconoclasistas, Andalgalá murals)

normative_stake.domain: political ontology / decolonial aesthetics / environmental politics

confidence_notes: The reflexive_move is true because the paper explicitly thematizes its own methodological position — the author reflects on the positionality of the ethnographer in relation to communities engaged in resistance, and notes the ethical dimensions of representing these practices. The case_function is 'generative' rather than 'illustrative' because the key concept (prefigurative ontological design) emerges from the fieldwork analysis rather than being pre-established and then confirmed. The epistemic mode is normative_with_qualitative_empirical with constitutive evidence rather than primarily_empirical because the theoretical-normative framework drives the analysis and the normative claim (that these practices enact an ontological shift that matters politically) is the paper's main contribution. The move_sequence includes method_justification because the paper devotes significant space to defending the ethnographic approach and its relation to political ontology theory — this is not background but an argumentative move establishing why this kind of knowledge is appropriate to the object.
